links.targetTitle: ACT uses per-grantee Diffie-Hellman key derivation with encrypted access keys in a manifest

global.title: Cross-user sharing rides Swarm ACT with an ECDH-derived per-session key instead of the wallet AES key
global.content:
Sharing with other users does NOT use the per-wallet AES key. Cross-user shares and chat history instead ride Swarm's Access Control Trie (ACT):

- The publisher's Bee node computes `ECDH(publisher_privkey × grantee_pubkey)` to derive a per-session key.
- A third party that only knows public addresses cannot derive the key (classic discrete-log problem).
- The Bee node handles encrypt/decrypt transparently; the app calls `uploadFile({ act: true, actHistoryAddress, skipEncryption: true })` on send and `downloadFile({ actPublisher, actHistoryAddress, skipDecryption: true })` on receive.

Share manifests and public profiles themselves stay unencrypted — they serve as discovery indexes so recipients can find what's shared with them. The data those indexes point to is ACT-protected.

This split matters for threat modeling: a grantee can decrypt what was shared with them, but revoking access means rotating ACT state, not rotating the wallet-derived sync key used for the owner's own operations.
global.description: Bee node computes ECDH(publisher_privkey × grantee_pubkey) to encrypt shared content; third parties cannot derive it from public addresses alone (discrete-log problem).
